# About this guide {.unnumbered}

This is the replication package for *Probabilistic Child Care Deserts: Bayesian
Accessibility Estimation with False Discovery Control in Alabama* (Lee, 2026).
Its one job is to let you reproduce every number, table, and figure in the paper
from runnable code and disclosure-safe shipped results — without taking any
result on faith.

The study starts from a deterministic child care **desert map** of Alabama — a
binary "desert / not desert" label for each census tract, computed from an
enhanced two-step floating catchment area (E2SFCA) access score. Binary maps like
this steer real public investment, but they are computed from uncertain inputs
and rarely say how sure any single designation is. This package builds the
machinery that turns that map into an **error-controlled decision system**:
it propagates the two dominant input uncertainties through a Bayesian spatial
model, gives each tract a posterior *probability* of being a desert, and then
declares deserts under procedures that control the false discovery rate.

The guide is deliberately reproduction-focused. It walks you from a clean R
session to the paper's exhibits and explains, at each step, both *how* the code
works and *why* each modeling choice was made. The deep scientific write-up —
the full argument and every result — lives in the paper itself; this guide is
the runnable companion to it.

## What this package reproduces

- **The posterior desert-probability map** for 1,409 Alabama census tracts
  (Figure F1), built from one Bayesian spatial fit plus 2,000 joint posterior
  draws that carry the demand and capacity uncertainties.
- **The error-controlled declarations**: 512 tracts declared at a false discovery
  rate of $q=0.10$, a stricter 412-tract exceedance-controlled core, and 13 of 67
  counties under a child-weighted rule (Figures F2, F3, F5).
- **The reclassification** of the deterministic map: of its 690 point deserts,
  500 survive the error control (72.5%), 190 lose declarable status without being
  exonerated, and 12 enter (Figure F4, Table T2).
- **The preregistered sensitivity analyses and the stability triangulation**
  (Figure F6, Table T3), and the aggregate interactive map.

Every headline value traces to a single registry, `outputs/key_numbers.csv`
(63 keys); the figures and tables read that registry rather than recomputing, and
`manifest/verify_outputs.R` checks the shipped artifacts against it.

## Two ways in

You do **not** need the confidential data to reproduce the exhibits. Pick the
track that matches what you have; @sec-getting-started covers both in full.

| Track | Starts from | Requires | Reproduces |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C — exhibits** *(default)* | the shipped aggregate layer `data/derived/` | R and CRAN packages only | every figure and the headline numbers |
| **A — full rerun** | the restricted inputs + a built companion P01 codebase | R, INLA, and the restricted data | every number, from raw sources |

Track C ships everything it needs and runs on a laptop in a minute. Track A
reruns the whole Bayesian pipeline and requires data governed by a confidential
data-use agreement (see `data/DATA_ACCESS.md`).

## How to read this guide

- @sec-overview — the study in brief: the problem, the approach, the findings.
- @sec-getting-started — set up R, choose a track, and run your first
  reproduction.
- @sec-inputs, @sec-model, @sec-posterior, @sec-declarations — the method, one
  stage at a time, with the code.
- @sec-exhibits — rebuild each figure and table, and tour the shipped results.
- @sec-reproducibility — seeds, determinism, verification, and data access.

## Citation and contact

Please cite the paper (see `CITATION.cff`). The package is released under the MIT
License; the underlying data are not distributed and are governed by separate
agreements (`data/DATA_ACCESS.md`).

**JoonHo Lee**, The University of Alabama — corresponding author and package
maintainer ([jlee296@ua.edu](mailto:jlee296@ua.edu), ORCID
[0009-0006-4019-8703](https://orcid.org/0009-0006-4019-8703)).
